root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Location Administration Guide
root.summary: Introductory front matter for the Cúram 8.1.2 Location Administration Guide. It identifies the guide’s purpose, intended audience (business analysts), prerequisites, and chapter map. The guide explains how organizations manage locations as a parent-child hierarchy, with only one active location structure at a time, and covers creating, activating, superseding, and canceling structures. It also previews location security and administration security using SIDs/access controls, plus general administration topics such as location resources, holiday calendars, daily schedules, translations of schedule slot names, and 14-day working patterns. The section ends with a summary and related guides, followed by legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory notice stating that before using the information and supported product, the reader should review the Notices section on page 21. It also shows the Merative US L.P. copyright years 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Overview of the Cúram Location Administration Guide. Covers the purpose, audience, prerequisites, and chapter map, then explains location structures and hierarchy, including root/parent-child relationships, list/tree navigation, and the rule that only one active structure can exist at a time. Also describes creating, translating, activating, superseding, and canceling structures. Introduces location security: SIDs for administration, data security levels (Off, On, Restricted View, Read Only), and how access is controlled by user, position, case owner, and primary client location.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-20
children.summary: Covers location data security modes and how additional location access changes visibility and maintenance rights across own, child, and assigned locations, including interaction with product SIDs. Describes general location administration: assigning resources, managing holiday calendars, daily schedules, translation of schedule slot names, and location working patterns. Explains key rules such as active resources preventing location deletion, schedules using slots and positions, and working patterns over a 14-day cycle. Ends with a summary of the guide’s main concepts and related guides.

Introductory and overview material for the Cúram Location Administration Guide. Covers purpose, audience, prerequisites, chapter map, and the main themes: location structures and hierarchy, one active structure at a time, lifecycle actions (create, activate, supersede, cancel), location administration security, location data security and product SIDs, and general location administration (resources, holiday calendars, daily schedules, translations, and working patterns). Useful as a roadmap before opening the detailed chapters.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section covers legal notices: fictitious sample data disclaimer, copyright/license terms for sample programs, warranty and liability limits, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, trademark notices for Merative and other vendors, and the copyright notice (2012, 2024). Useful for checking usage rights, privacy policy access, and trademark attributions.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists trademark and copyright notices for the document and related software. It states that sample names are fictitious, sample programs are provided “as is” under Merative’s client agreement, and gives the privacy policy URL. It also identifies trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX, and notes that other names may be trademarks of others.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication. Covers permitted personal and enterprise use, preservation of proprietary notices, restrictions on redistribution and derivative works, export-law compliance, and Merative’s right to withdraw permissions. States the content is provided “as-is” with no warranties, no patent license is granted, and Merative is not პასუხისმგible for third-party products or websites. Includes the copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Location Administration Guide. Lists the guide’s main sections: introduction, location structures, managing the location structure, location security, general location administration, and conclusion, with subtopics such as location hierarchy, security, creating/activating/superseding structures, resources, holiday calendars, daily schedules, and working patterns. Also includes front matter like notes, edition, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation entry rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Cúram Location Administration Guide
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: Introduces the Cúram Location Administration Guide and its purpose: managing an organization’s locations, location structures, location security, and resources. Explains that locations are organized in a parent-child hierarchy, with only one active location structure at a time. Also states the intended audience (business analysts), prerequisites, and the guide’s main chapters on location structures and managing the location structure.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business analysts with strong knowledge of the organization’s business requirements and only basic application knowledge. The guide explains location administration in Cúram, including location structures, location security, resources, and management of organizational locations and reporting hierarchy. It assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management, the Organization Administration Guide, and basic system administration concepts. It also outlines the guide’s chapters on location structures and managing the location structure.

children.title: Prerequisites
children.summary: This section introduces the Cúram Location Administration Guide and states the prerequisites for readers. It explains that locations are organizational work sites, managed through a parent-child location structure, with only one active structure at a time. The guide is aimed at business analysts and assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management, organizational location/security administration, reporting hierarchy, and the Organization Administration Guide; it may also be used with the System Configuration Guide. It also previews chapters on location structures and managing them.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This introduction to the Cúram Location Administration Guide explains that locations are organizational workplaces managed through a single active parent-child location structure. It outlines the guide’s purpose: administering locations, location structures, location security, and resources, with a focus on partitioning administration across the organization. It identifies the audience as business analysts, assumes familiarity with social enterprise administration and the Organization Administration Guide, and points to the next chapters on location structures and managing the location structure.

Introduction to the Cúram Location Administration Guide. It explains that locations are organizational workplaces organized in a parent-child hierarchy, with only one active location structure at a time. The guide covers administration of locations, location structures, location security, and resources, with emphasis on partitioning administration across the organization. Intended for business analysts with strong business knowledge and basic application understanding, it assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management and the Organization Administration Guide. It also previews chapters on location structures and managing the location structure.

children.title: 1.2 Location Structures
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Location Structures chapter. Explains that a location structure manages an organization’s locations, supporting parent/child hierarchies in tree or standard views, with multiple structures allowed but only one active at a time. Covers what locations represent, their shared address and operating status, and that structures must be activated before use. Also notes location security for controlling case/client access, plus location holidays, resources, schedules, and admin security via security identifiers.

children.title: Locations and the Location Structure
children.summary: Explains the organization’s location structure and what locations represent. Covers locations as work or public offices, address and operating status, parent/child hierarchies, tree and standard views, and adding locations from either view. Notes that multiple location structures can exist but only one can be active at a time, and activation replaces the current active structure. Also introduces location security and mentions related data such as holidays, resources, schedules, and administrative security identifiers.

children.title: Location Administration Security
children.summary: Introduces location structures and administration security. Explains that locations can be organized hierarchically, viewed in tree or standard application forms, and activated as the current structure; only one active structure can exist at a time. Notes key location attributes such as address, operating status, holidays, resources, and schedules. Also states that location security can restrict access to case/client information, and that location administration details can be secured with security identifiers, with more detail in the referenced location security section.

children.title: Understanding the Location Hierarchy
The Root Location
children.summary: Explains the location hierarchy and the root location in Cúram. There is one root location per organization structure, always at the top, and any location beneath it can be a parent. Location hierarchy supports security at both administration and case/client levels. Describes parent/child relationships, notes that location type does not affect hierarchy placement, and outlines list view vs. tree view navigation. Also mentions tabs for Home, Tree View, Locations, and Additional Details, plus actions like editing, adding child locations, assigning access, and deleting locations.

children.title: Parent/Child Locations
children.summary: Explains how location hierarchy works for security and organization structure. Covers parent/child relationships, the single root location, and that location type does not affect hierarchy placement. Describes two navigation modes for the location structure: list view and tree view, including drilling down, expanding/collapsing nodes, and viewing location details. Notes available pages/tabs (Home, Tree View, Locations, Additional Details) and key actions such as creating child locations, assigning access, editing, deleting, and creating schedules.

Explains the organization’s location hierarchy in Cúram: one root location per structure, with parent/child relationships determining placement and supporting security at administration and case/client levels. Location type does not affect hierarchy position. Describes two navigation modes—list view and tree view—along with tabs for Home, Tree View, Locations, and Additional Details. Notes that users can inspect location details, expand/collapse nodes, drill down the tree, and perform actions such as editing, adding child locations, assigning access, deleting locations, and creating schedules.

children.title: Navigating the Location Structure
children.summary: Explains the location hierarchy and how locations relate as parent/child nodes under a single root location. Describes two ways to browse the structure: list view and tree view. List view uses tabs and expandable items; tree view shows the hierarchy, lets users drill down, expand/collapse nodes, and open a location’s home page. Covers tabs available on the location structure (Home, Tree View, Locations, Additional Details) and key actions such as adding/editing locations, creating child locations and schedules, assigning access, and deleting locations.

Introduces the Location Structures chapter and explains how an organization’s locations are managed in a hierarchical structure. Locations may be work places or public offices, each with an address and operating status; multiple locations can share an address. The structure supports parent/child relationships, tree and standard views, and adding locations from either view. Multiple structures can exist, but only one may be active and must be activated before use. Also covers location security, location administration security identifiers, and related data such as holidays, resources, and schedules.

children.title: 1.3 Managing the Location Structure
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces location structures in Cúram 8.1.2, explaining that they define relationships between organization locations and can be active, in edit, superseded, or canceled. It outlines that a location structure must be created before locations can be defined, starts with a root location, and can be viewed in tree or non-tree form. It also covers translating location names, including when multilingual support is available and the steps to add or edit translations through the Administration Workspace.

children.title: Creating a Location Structure
Translating Location Names
children.summary: Explains how to translate location names in a Cúram location structure for multilingual applications. Covers when translation text is needed, notes runtime language selection based on user locale, and mentions that single-language non-English deployments can edit the location name directly. Gives the admin procedure to navigate to a location structure, open a location, use Translate Text, add a new translation, or edit an existing one.

Explains how to create a location structure in Cúram before locations can be defined, with the root location added first and child locations added beneath it. Notes that a new location structure starts in “in edit” status and can be viewed in tree or non-tree form. Also covers translating location names for multilingual deployments: translations are shown based on user locale, and admins can add or edit translations via Administration Workspace > Organization > My Organization > Structures > Location Structures > location > Translate Text.

children.title: Activating the Location Structure
children.summary: Explains how to activate a location structure and what happens afterward: only one active organization/location structure can exist at a time, activation assigns an activation date, and only in edit or superseded structures can be activated. Activated structures supersede the previous active one, while canceled structures cannot be activated. Also covers superseding and canceling: only in edit or superseded structures can be deleted/canceled. Ends by introducing location security, including how hierarchical locations restrict access and how Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs control location administration permissions.

children.title: Superseding and Canceling the Location Structure
children.summary: This section explains how location structures are activated, superseded, and canceled. Only one active organization/location structure can exist at a time; activating an in edit or superseded structure assigns an activation date and makes it replace the previous active structure. Canceled structures cannot be activated. It also notes that only in edit or superseded structures can be deleted/canceled, since they are no longer in use.

Covers how to manage a location structure in Cúram: create it before defining locations, start with a root location, add child locations, and view the hierarchy in tree or list form. Explains that structures can be active, in edit, superseded, or canceled, and that location names can be translated for multilingual deployments via Administration Workspace. Also describes how the UI shows hierarchy and how tree/list views support navigation and editing.

children.title: 1.4 Location Security
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces location administration security and how the hierarchical location structure controls access to case and client data. Explains that each location has Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs: Create is needed to add child locations, Read to view administration data for a location and its sublocations, and Maintain to edit administration data. Also notes that maintain access includes view rights, and that location security can restrict or extend access beyond a user’s own location.

children.title: Location Administration Security
children.summary: Explains location administration security in Cúram. Each location is assigned Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs: Create allows creating child locations, Read allows viewing administration data for the location and sublocations, and Maintain allows maintaining administration data for the location and sublocations. Maintain SIDs also grant view access to administration data. This section sits within broader location security, which limits access to case/client information through the hierarchical location structure.

children.title: Understanding Location Security
children.summary: Explains Cúram location security, which controls access to case/client data based on user location, user position, case owner, and primary client location. Distinguishes location data security from product location security. Describes configuration via the organization home page and the four location data security modes: Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only, including their access effects. Notes exceptions: case owners/supervisors always have access, and clients with no location are visible to all users.

children.title: Using Location Based Security to authorize User Access
Location Data Security set to 'Off'
children.summary: Explains location security in Cúram and how the organization-wide location data security setting controls access to case and client records. Focuses on the “Off” setting: when disabled, users can view and maintain all cases and client details regardless of their own location. Also notes broader location security concepts, including comparisons against user, case owner, and primary client locations, plus other available modes (On, Restricted View, Read Only).

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'On'
children.summary: Explains how Cúram location data security affects case/client access by location. Covers the three base modes: On (access only own location and sub-locations), Restricted View (can see other locations exist but not details), and Read Only (can view other locations but not maintain). Also describes how additional location access expands rights, including inherited child locations, apply-to-sub-locations behavior, read vs maintain permissions, and examples such as call centers and users with multiple positions.

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'Restricted View'
children.summary: Explains Cúram location data security behavior, focusing on how “Restricted View” limits users to viewing/maintaining cases and clients in their own location and sub-locations while only seeing that records exist elsewhere. Also compares this mode with “On” and “Read Only,” including how additional location access and sub-location inheritance can expand view or maintain rights across locations, with examples such as call centers and users with multiple positions.

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'Read Only'
children.summary: Explains Cúram location data security modes and how they affect access to cases/clients by location and sub-locations. For “Read Only,” users can view and maintain records in their own and sub-locations, and can read but not maintain records in other locations. Also covers interactions with additional location access, including when maintain rights extend to other locations and child locations, and notes that read-only additional access is redundant because viewing is already allowed.

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'On' with additional access assigned.
children.summary: Explains how Cúram location data security behaves when set to On, Restricted View, or Read Only, including what users can view or maintain in their own location and sub-locations. Also covers additional location access: users may gain view/maintain rights for assigned locations and child locations, optionally inherited by sub-locations, with examples such as call centers and users with multiple positions. Includes notes on read-rights versus maintain-rights combinations and how they affect access.

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'Read Only' with additional access assigned.
children.summary: Explains how Cúram location data security behaves when set to Read Only, including baseline access and the effect of additional location access. Users can view and maintain records in their own and sub-locations, and with added maintain rights can maintain records in granted locations and their children. Notes that read-only additional access is unnecessary under this mode. Also continues into Restricted View with additional access, indicating permissions can be extended by location and sub-location, including cases where users hold multiple positions.

children.title: Location Data Security set to 'Restricted View' with additional access assigned.
children.summary: Explains Cúram location data security behaviors, especially “Restricted View” and its interaction with additional location access. Covers what users can see vs maintain in their own and other locations, including child/sub-locations, read-only vs maintain rights, and how administrators can grant access to extra locations (optionally cascading to sub-locations). Also notes multiple positions in different locations and that read-rights additional access is redundant under Read Only.

Explains Cúram location-based security for authorizing case/client access by comparing user, position, case owner, and primary client locations. Defines the organization-wide Location Data Security modes Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only, and their baseline access rules. Also covers additional location access, including maintain vs read rights, child/sub-location inheritance, multiple user positions, and examples like call centers. Useful for understanding how location settings control view/maintain permissions across the organization.

children.title: Product Security Administration
children.summary: Explains product security as an added layer on top of location data security for securing cases and products using SID functionality. Cases can be restricted by location only or by both location and product settings; with location security on, access is limited to a user’s own location and sub-locations, and product SIDs can further narrow access. Useful for understanding how case access is controlled by organization security settings.

Covers Cúram location security for case/client access and location administration. Explains that each location has Create, Read, and Maintain SIDs for hierarchical location administration, with Maintain including view rights. Describes organization-level location data security modes Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only, how they affect view/maintain access, and how access is compared using user/position, case owner, and primary client location. Also notes exceptions for case owners/supervisors, clients with no location, and additional location access inheritance.

children.title: 1.5 General Location Administration
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces general location administration in Cúram 8.1.2, covering assigning resources to locations, maintaining holiday calendars, daily schedules, and resources. Explains location resources (e.g., printers, conference rooms), that they can be configured, assigned to one or multiple locations, and applied to child locations if active/operating. Notes resources have parameters and require sufficient admin rights to view or maintain, including child-location permissions. Also briefly covers security: location data security combined with product SID security can further restrict access to cases and clients.

children.title: Location Resources
children.summary: Defines location resources and how they’re managed in Cúram. Resources are location-specific tools, equipment, or office spaces (e.g., printers, conference rooms) whose types are configurable. Existing active/operating resources can be assigned to one or multiple locations, including child locations. A location cannot be deleted if it has an active resource. Resources may have configurable parameters such as network addresses or serial codes. Access requires sufficient location administration rights, including maintain rights for child locations.

children.title: Location Holiday Calendar
children.summary: Explains location holiday calendars and daily schedules. Holidays are maintained at the location level because different locations may observe different closures, and only users with sufficient location administration rights can view or assign them, including to child locations. Daily schedules define time slots for a location, with a required work type, slot allocations, and assigned positions/users used to determine how many work items can be scheduled. Includes an example of interview capacity and notes that slot names can be translated or edited for supported languages.

children.title: Location Daily Schedules
Translating Location Schedule Slot Names
children.summary: Explains how to add or edit translation text for location schedule slot names in Cúram. It applies to multilingual applications, with runtime language chosen by the user’s locale; if only one non-English language is supported, the slot name can be edited directly in that language. Also briefly covers related location administration context for holiday calendars and daily schedules, including access rights and schedule slots. The procedure begins in Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization, then Structures > Location Structures.

Explains location daily schedules and holiday calendars in Cúram. Holiday calendars track closed days at the location level, and daily schedules define work time slots, slot allocations, and assigned positions/users for scheduling work. Access requires sufficient location administration rights. Also notes that schedule slot names can be translated or edited for multilingual support, with runtime language chosen by user locale. Includes the navigation path to administer location structures: Administration Workspace > Shortcuts > Organization > My Organization > Structures > Location Structures.

children.title: Location Working Patterns
children.summary: This section explains location working patterns for organization locations. It describes standard 14-day schedules starting Monday and repeating indefinitely if no end date is set, plus non-standard and non-working days for irregular hours. It also distinguishes working hours and public opening hours pattern types, where the latter defines when a public office is open to the public. Only one active pattern of each type is allowed in a given period.

Cúram 8.1.2 general location administration covers managing location resources, holiday calendars, daily schedules, and working patterns from the location administration home page. Resources are configurable, location-specific assets such as printers or rooms, can be assigned to multiple locations and child locations, require active/operating status, and may have parameters. Holiday calendars track location closures. Daily schedules define time slots, work types, capacity, and assigned positions/users, with translation support for slot names. Working patterns define 14-day repeating schedules, non-standard days, and public opening hours.

children.title: 1.6 Conclusion
Summary
children.summary: This section summarizes the guide’s key points: managing an organization’s location structure, including its lifecycle (create, activate, supersede, cancel); using location and position to limit access to case and client information; maintaining location resources, holidays, and daily schedules; and applying location security and administration security to restrict access as needed. It also points readers to related guides for broader system, organization, participant, and case management concepts.

children.title: Additional Information
children.summary: This section closes the guide with a brief recap of location administration concepts and points to related guides: System Administration, Organization Administration, Participant, and Integrated Case Management. It then provides legal notices covering usage permissions, copyright and export restrictions, warranty disclaimers, sample code terms, privacy policy link, and trademark information for Merative and other vendors.

This conclusion recaps the guide’s main topics: managing the organization’s location structure and its lifecycle (create, activate, supersede, cancel), using location and position to restrict access to case/client data, maintaining location resources, holidays, and daily schedules, and applying location security plus administration security. It also points to related guides on System Administration, Organization Administration, Participant, and Integrated Case Management. The remaining pages contain legal notices, usage permissions, warranty/disclaimer text, sample code terms, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Overview of Cúram location administration: locations are organizational workplaces/public offices arranged in a parent-child hierarchy, with only one active location structure at a time. Covers creating, activating, superseding, and canceling structures; navigating tree/list views; translating location names; and applying location and administration security via SIDs and access modes. Also describes managing holidays, resources, daily schedules, and 14-day working patterns. Concludes with related guides and legal notices.
